full_text:
Development and Optimization of High-Grade Serous Ovarian Carcinoma Tissue Processing: Focus on 3D Culture of Cancer-Associated Fibroblasts

Mid-phase presentation 13.1.2025
Aino Elomaa

Supervisors:
Assistant Professor, Anniina Färkkilä, University of Helsinki
Doctoral Researcher, María Hincapié Otero, University of Helsinki
Professor, Ullamari Pesonen, University of Turku

High grade serous ovarian carcinoma (HGSC)

2

Created with BioRender.com

 Need for a deeper understanding of the HGSC’s biological complexity

Speaker notes:
most common subtype, accounting 70% of epithelial ovarian cancers

also the most aggressive subtype, typically diagnosed at advanced stages with metastasis to omentum and surrounding abdominal organs

despite initial response the treatment, relapse is common  around 25% of patients experience cancer recurrence within six months after treatment

highlights urged need for new therapeutical approaches  required deeper understanding of the HGSC’s biological complexity  and TME has gained increasing attention in its role in determining clinical outcomes

Cancer-associated fibroblasts (CAFs)

3

Created with BioRender.com

Speaker notes:
Within the TME, CAFs are a highly heterogenous population

A high amounts of CAFs in epithelial ovarian cancer tumors is linked to advanced-stage disease such as the presence of lymph node and omentum metastases. 

CAFs contribute to tumor growth and behavior by promoting metastasis and angiogenesis, as well as affecting immune cell infiltration and modulating immune responses

Therefore there is a need to have a model that represents the TME and which incorporates CAFs

My thesis project aims to contribute to the Färkkilä laboratory's efforts to establish and validate a novel 3D tri-culture model of HGSC

4

Created with BioRender.com

Speaker notes:
labs aim to establish and validate a novel 3D tri-culture model of HGSC

The lab has already optimized an immunocompetent cell culture model that supports the growth of cancer and immune cells  but now the aim is incorporated CAFs

Aim is to use freshly dissociated tumor cells and embed them in omentum-derived gel to have a TME-representative model  This model could be used for drug testing to identify novel effective drug combinations and for biomarker discovery

My project aims to:

5

Speaker notes:
To remind, my thesis aims to

Preliminary results

6

Aim 1: Establish a standardized protocol for processing HGSC tissue samples

7

Speaker notes:
1) establish standardized protocol for tissue processing with the focus of successful dissociation of CAFs  testing 3 different enzymatic dissociations *** and evaluated the dissociation method based on quantifying the yield of CAFs, using flow cytometry

Gating strategy for flow cytometry analysis

8

Speaker notes:
Before showing the results, I will briefly explain how the flow cytometry data is analyzed:

First, I gate on cells to exclude debris, which are small particles or cell fragments not relevant to the analysis.

Next, I select single cells to ensure I’m analyzing individual cells, not doublets or aggregates.

From the single-cell population, I gate the live cells, excluding any dead cells.

Gating strategy for flow cytometry analysis

9

EpCAM+ = Cancer cells
CD45+ = Leukocytes
EpCAM- CD45- = Non-immune stroma
EpCAM- CD45- CD31+ = Endothelial cells
EpCAM- CD45- CD31- FAP+/a-SMA+/PDRGF-b+/CD29+ = CAFs

Speaker notes:
Within the live cells, I use EpCAM and CD45 markers to distinguish cancer cells (EpCAM+) and leukocytes (CD45+).

From the CD45-negative and EpCAM-negative population, which represents the non-immune stroma, I exclude endothelial cells using the CD31 marker.

Finally, from the CD31-negative population, I identify fibroblasts based on markers like FAP, α-SMA, PDGFR-β, and CD29.

This approach allows me to focus on the specific cell populations of interest while excluding irrelevant or overlapping populations.  then to the results

10

Enzymatic dissociation using Dispase and Collagenase & Hyaluronidase sequentially improves the yield of CAFs

Speaker notes:
By analyzing the frequency of FAP pos cell between dissociation methods and samples, we can see that the third dissociation method (Dispase and C&H sequentially) improves the yield of CAF 

On the x-axis you can see the different dissociation methods and within all samples, the sequential dissociation results in more CAFs.

11

Enzymatic dissociation using Dispase and Collagenase & Hyaluronidase sequentially improves the yield of CAFs

 Optimized SOP for HGSC tissue sample processing using the sequential enzymatic dissociation

Speaker notes:
So I have optimized a stadardized protocol for HGSC tissue processing which enables sufficient dissociation of CAFs

Aim 2: Characterize key cell types within the HGSC TME

12

Speaker notes:
2) characterize the key cell types  While quantifying the CAFs abundance within the sample using different dissociation methods, I also acquired data from the cellular composition within the samples

13

Frequencies of EpCAM⁺ cancer cells and CD45⁺ leukocytes across dissociation methods

* indicates p-value < 0.05

Speaker notes:
Looking at the frequencies of cancer cells and leukocytes  there are statistical differences between cancer cells when comparing the sequential dissociation to the individual dissociations 
BUT using the different dissociation methods does not significantly alter the yield of leukocytes

14

Frequency of CD31⁺ endothelial cells shows some statistical differences across dissociation methods

* indicates p-value < 0.05

Speaker notes:
Looking at the frequency of endothelial cells  again there is a some statistical difference but not between all dissociation methods

15

Frequency of CAFs shows statistical differences across dissociation methods

* indicates p-value < 0.05

Speaker notes:
Moving on to the CAFs  When again looking at the frequency of FAP pos cells, there is a statistically significance difference between the dissociation methods  sequential dissociation improves the yield of CAFs

Similarly with another fibroblast marker a-SMA, there is a statistically significant difference between dissociation methods


FAP = fibroblast activation protein
aSMA = α-smooth muscle actin
PDGRFb = platelet-derived growth factor receptor beta
CD29 = integrin β1: cell adhesion, migration, and signaling

16

Frequency of fibroblasts shows statistical differences across dissociation methods

* indicates p-value < 0.05

Speaker notes:
Also, PDGRFb and CD29 can be used to characterize CAFs

Again, similar results with PDGRFb as there is a statistically significant difference between dissociation methods

However, statistically significant results are not seen with CD29 but similar trend can be seen


FAP = fibroblast activation protein
aSMA = α-smooth muscle actin
PDGRFb = platelet-derived growth factor receptor beta
CD29 = integrin β1: cell adhesion, migration, and signaling

Aim 3: Enrich CAFs from HGSC patient samples

17

Speaker notes:
3) enrich CAFs from the acquired cell suspension using magnetic-activated cell sorting (MACS)

briefly about this technique: its used to isolate specific cell populations based on their surface markers. So here, the aim is to enrich CAFs by labeling cells with magnetic beads conjugated to antibodies targeting fibroblast-specific markers. When passed through a magnetic column, CAFs are retained, while unlabeled cells flow through. The retained, labeled cells can then be eluted from the column for downstream analysis.

Optimization of MACS protocol needed for effective CAF enrichment

18

Speaker notes:
Tried to enrich CAFs with this technique and here are the results. 

This figure shows flow cytometry plots illustrating the distribution of cell populations before enrichment, in the column-bound fraction, and in the flowthrough. The aim was to enrich CAFs which are part of the CD45 neg, EpCAM neg population. And theoretically the column-bound sample should include only fibroblasts.

Optimization of MACS protocol needed for effective CAF enrichment

19

Speaker notes:
Within the column-bound sample, the CD45- EpCAM- cell are only 45% of the cell suspension. This is suboptimal as optimally the yield of target population should be around 90 %

As seen in the flowthrough sample, a portion of the target population remains in the flowthrough. 

To conclude, this MACS protocol needs troubleshooting

Aim 4: Compare the growth and behavior of patient-derived CAFs in different environments

20

Speaker notes:
Compare the growth and behavior of patient-derived CAFs in different environments. Plan was to use MACS to enrich CAFs from the cell suspension 

 BUT since the MACS did not perform optimally, we decided to plate the dissociated cells in traditional 2D culture to enrich the CAF population.

Under traditional 2D culture conditions, CAFs tend to outgrow other cell types, making them the predominant population over time. 

And then I plated them in different conditions and compared CAFs growth in omentum-gel, Basement membrane extract gel and in suspension culture.

21

Suspension, Cultrex-gel and Omentum-gel support the formation of CAF spheroids

Speaker notes:
I plated CAFs in suspension and embedded in both omentum-gel and Cultrex-gel which is a type of commercially available BME. 

To see if the cell density effects the spheroid formation, I used 3 different cell densities  And monitored cell growth over 10 days

As can be seen, all three conditions support the formation of spheroid.

22

Lower concentration of Cultrex-gel advantage in spheroid formation

Speaker notes:
In addition to that, I wanted to see if the gel density effects the spheroid formations

Used Cultrex in three different concentrations, and observed, that using higher concentration of cultrex meaning a more dense gel the cells start to outgrow from the formed spheroid and a less dense gel supports the cells growth within the spheroid. Between the intermediate and low concentration Cultrex, the formed spheroids look similar and well structured.

23

Different Omentum-gel densities support the spheroid formation

Speaker notes:
When comparing different densities of omentum-gel, a similar outgrowing of CAFs from the gel droplet is not seen. However, the spheroid structure seems more consistent with the Cultrex-gel compared to spheroids in Cultrex-gel.

Evaluation of the project progress

24

Speaker notes:
To conclude the presentation, few words on evaluation of the project progress. 

I’m continuing with lab work with the third and four aim but the focus is shifting toward writing the thesis. I’m going to try the MACS sorting again with few alterations to the protocol. And overall, the results still needs to be evaluated to determine the final conclusions.

Thank you! Questions?

25
